attributes.stem:
The junior doctor on a general medical team is asked to review a patient after her morning bloods revealed a hyponatraemia with a sodium of 128 mmol/L (135-145).

The patient is a 72-year-old woman currently admitted under general medicine for investigation of presyncope. She has a history of depression, osteoarthritis, and hypertension. Her medications include sertraline, paracetamol, perindopril, amlodipine, and esomeprazole, all of which she has continued to receive regularly on the ward. She does not smoke or drink alcohol.

On examination, she appears euvolaemic with vital signs within normal limits. There are no focal neurological signs, and she reports feeling like her usual self.

Further bloods are added on to investigate the hyponatraemia, with results revealing:

  • Sodium 128 mmol/L (135-145)

  • Potassium 4.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)

  • Serum osmolality 265 mOsm/kg (285-295)

  • Urine osmolality 820 mOsm/kg (300-900)

  • Urine sodium 200 mmol/L (40-220)

  • Thyroid stimulating hormone 2.1 mU/L (0.4-4.0)

  • Morning cortisol 380 nmol/L (140-690)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the patient's medications is most likely contributing to the hyponatraemia?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with hyponatraemia with biochemistry suggestive of syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH). The key findings are hyponatraemia with low serum osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine (elevated urine osmolality), and elevated urine sodium in a euvolaemic patient. Other causes of SIADH have been excluded with normal thyroid and cortisol levels.

Sertraline, a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI), is a well-known cause of SIADH, particularly in elderly patients. SSRIs can increase antidiuretic hormone (ADH) release and enhance its effects at the kidney, leading to water retention and hyponatraemia.

Perindopril, an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, rarely causes hyponatraemia. While it can affect kidney function, it typically does not cause SIADH.

Paracetamol does not affect sodium homeostasis and is not associated with hyponatraemia.

Amlodipine is not typically associated with hyponatraemia and does not cause SIADH.

Esomeprazole has been only rarely associated with hyponatraemia, but it is a much less likely cause than SSRIs.

Management would involve reviewing the need for sertraline, likely withholding the medication or changing to an alternative antidepressant, along with fluid restriction while the medication changes take effect.
learningPoints: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are a common cause of drug-induced syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH).
Medication review is an essential component of investigating hyponatraemia.
The elderly are particularly susceptible to drug-induced hyponatraemia.
references: Dineen R, Thompson CJ, Sherlock M. Hyponatraemia - presentations and management. Clin Med (Lond). 2017 Jun;17(3):263-269. doi: 10.7861/clinmedicine.17-3-263. PMID: 28572229; PMCID: PMC6297575.

Rondon H, Badireddy M. Hyponatremia. [Updated 2023 Jun 14]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470386/

Yasir M, Mechanic OJ. Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion. [Updated 2023 Mar 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK507777/
